narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.30, an LSAT score of 36.50, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school deciles from Year 1 and Year 3. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 52.7% chance that you would fail the bar on your first attempt—tipping just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction isn't a judgment on your potential or character, but a data-driven assessment comparing your profile to others who have taken the bar. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which is notably lower than the typical student's 5.53 average and signaled to the model a potential challenge in sustaining strong academic performance heading into law school. Next in importance, your undergraduate GPA of 3.30 worked in your favor—above the dataset average of 3.21—pulling the prediction toward bar exam success and showing the model your solid college foundation. Your Year 1 decile of 3, however, also contributed toward the failure side, as it fell below the average of 5.71 and highlighted an early academic hurdle compared to peers. Overall, your LSAT score was right around the average of 36.70 and didn't sway the outcome much, while your full-time status aligned with the vast majority of students (over 92%) who pursued that path. These top three academic markers—especially the weaker later decile—outweighed your strengths in this close call, mirroring patterns where students with similar dips in school rankings struggled more on the bar. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, as even small improvements there could shift future predictions dramatically. You might also seek targeted LSAT or GPA-enhancing prep, or explore schools with holistic reviews that weigh your Hispanic background and upper-middle stability more personally. Many students in your position have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps—your full-time commitment and above-average undergrad record give you a strong base to build on. Keep pushing; this is just one model's snapshot, not your story's end.
